top_papers.title: TriAttention: Efficient Long Reasoning with Trigonometric KV Compression
top_papers.summary: Extended reasoning in large language models (LLMs) creates severe KV cache memory bottlenecks. Leading KV cache compression methods estimate KV importance using attention scores from recent post-RoPE queries. However, queries rotate with position during RoPE, making representative queries very few, leading to poor top-key selection and unstable reasoning. To avoid this issue, we turn to the pre-RoPE space, where we observe that Q and K vectors are highly concentrated around fixed non-zero centers and remain stable across positions -- Q/K concentration. We show that this concentration causes queries to preferentially attend to keys at specific distances (e.g., nearest keys), with the centers determining which distances are preferred via a trigonometric series. Based on this, we propose TriAttention to estimate key importance by leveraging these centers. Via the trigonometric series, we use the distance preference characterized by these centers to score keys according to their positions, and also leverage Q/K norms as an additional signal for importance estimation. On AIME25 with 32K-token generation, TriAttention matches Full Attention reasoning accuracy while achieving 2.5x higher throughput or 10.7x KV memory reduction, whereas leading baselines achieve only about half the accuracy at the same efficiency. TriAttention enables OpenClaw deployment on a single consumer GPU, where long context would otherwise cause out-of-memory with Full Attention.

ai.one_liner_zh: This paper proposes a RoPE-aware KV cache compression method that scores key importance from pre-RoPE Q/K structure instead of recent post-RoPE attention, targeting long-reasoning memory bottlenecks.
ai.summary_zh: This paper studies KV cache compression for long-reasoning LLM workloads, where memory growth and cache bandwidth become the main deployment bottlenecks. The key claim is that post-RoPE queries are a poor basis for importance estimation, so the method moves to pre-RoPE space and uses stable Q/K centers plus a trigonometric distance-preference model, along with Q/K norms, to rank keys for retention. The abstract reports that on AIME25 with 32K-token generation, the method matches full-attention reasoning accuracy while delivering either higher throughput or much lower KV memory use, and that it makes single-consumer-GPU deployment feasible for a long-context OpenClaw setup. The practical value is clear if the scoring rule is cheap and robust, because it would turn KV compression from a heuristic cache pruning trick into a more structure-aware long-context serving mechanism. The main missing details are baseline fairness, runtime overhead of the scoring logic, hardware setup, and how well the claimed Q/K concentration behavior generalizes beyond the reported setting.
ai.background_zh: Long-context reasoning pushes KV cache memory and bandwidth to the forefront, so inference systems need compression schemes that preserve accuracy under aggressive cache reduction.
ai.problem_zh: The paper asks how to estimate KV importance more reliably under RoPE, given that recent post-RoPE attention can mis-rank keys and destabilize long reasoning.
ai.approach_zh: It models stable pre-RoPE Q/K concentration around non-zero centers, derives position-based distance preferences through a trigonometric series, and combines that signal with Q/K norms to score which keys to keep.
ai.evidence_zh: The abstract claims matching full-attention accuracy on AIME25 at 32K tokens while improving throughput or reducing KV memory substantially, but it does not make the evaluation protocol, hardware assumptions, or per-baseline cost tradeoffs fully clear.
ai.value_zh: If the method holds up, it could materially improve long-context LLM serving by reducing KV footprint enough to unlock cheaper hardware targets without giving up reasoning quality.
open_questions: What is the exact runtime overhead of TriAttention's scoring pipeline relative to the saved KV memory and bandwidth?
How fair are the baseline comparisons in token budget, retained-cache ratio, decoding setup, and hardware configuration?
Does the pre-RoPE concentration assumption remain stable across model families, layers, tasks, and much longer contexts than the reported AIME25 setting?
core_contributions: Identifies post-RoPE query instability as a weakness in existing KV importance estimation for long reasoning.
Introduces a pre-RoPE key scoring method based on Q/K concentration centers, trigonometric distance preference, and Q/K norms.
Reports a deployment-oriented result where long-context reasoning fits on a single consumer GPU instead of running out of memory under full attention.
why_read: It is directly about KV-cache compression for long-reasoning inference, which is a core systems bottleneck.
The mechanism is more structural than standard attention-score heuristics and may matter for robust cache pruning under RoPE.
The abstract includes both accuracy and deployment claims, so it is useful for judging whether this is a real serving method or just a benchmark-specific compression trick.
risks: The claimed gains may depend heavily on the specific benchmark, context length, or model rather than general long-context serving workloads.

The abstract does not clarify the computational overhead of the scoring method, so throughput gains may be sensitive to implementation details and hardware balance between compute and memory bandwidth.
recommended_for: Researchers working on KV-cache compression and long-context LLM inference.
Engineers evaluating whether structured cache scoring can reduce memory enough for consumer-GPU deployment.
Readers interested in how RoPE geometry can inform inference-time systems decisions.
ai.reading_priority_reason: High fit with LLM inference systems: it targets KV-cache memory directly, claims substantial throughput and memory gains, and proposes a concrete mechanism tied to RoPE behavior rather than a generic pruning heuristic.
ai.fallback_summary: Extended reasoning in large language models (LLMs) creates severe KV cache memory bottlenecks.

top_papers.title: GENSERVE: Efficient Co-Serving of Heterogeneous Diffusion Model Workloads
top_papers.summary: Diffusion models have emerged as the prevailing approach for text-to-image (T2I) and text-to-video (T2V) generation, yet production platforms must increasingly serve both modalities on shared GPU clusters while meeting stringent latency SLOs. Co-serving such heterogeneous workloads is challenging: T2I and T2V requests exhibit vastly different compute demands, parallelism characteristics, and latency requirements, leading to significant SLO violations in existing serving systems. We present GENSERVE, a co-serving system that leverages the inherent predictability of the diffusion process to optimize serving efficiency. A central insight is that diffusion inference proceeds in discrete, predictable steps and is naturally preemptible at step boundaries, opening a new design space for heterogeneity-aware resource management. GENSERVE introduces step-level resource adaptation through three coordinated mechanisms: intelligent video preemption, elastic sequence parallelism with dynamic batching, and an SLO-aware scheduler that jointly optimizes resource allocation across all concurrent requests. Experimental results show that GENSERVE improves the SLO attainment rate by up to 44% over the strongest baseline across diverse configurations.

ai.one_liner_zh: This paper proposes a diffusion serving system that co-schedules text-to-image and text-to-video requests by exploiting step-level predictability and preemptibility in diffusion inference.
ai.summary_zh: This paper targets a practical serving problem: text-to-image and text-to-video diffusion workloads increasingly share GPU clusters, but their compute profiles and latency needs differ enough that existing systems miss SLOs. The core idea is to treat diffusion inference as a sequence of predictable, discrete steps, then use that structure for finer-grained resource control during co-serving. GENSERVE combines video preemption at step boundaries, elastic sequence parallelism with dynamic batching, and an SLO-aware scheduler that jointly allocates resources across concurrent requests. The abstract claims up to a 44% improvement in SLO attainment rate over a strongest baseline across diverse settings, which suggests the gains come from better scheduling and adaptation rather than a new model architecture. The main missing details are hardware assumptions, workload mix, baseline fairness, and the overheads of preemption and dynamic reconfiguration.
ai.background_zh: Shared GPU serving for diffusion models is becoming more common as platforms support both image and video generation, but these workloads stress the cluster in different ways.
ai.problem_zh: The paper asks how to co-serve heterogeneous diffusion requests without causing excessive latency or SLO violations when T2I and T2V jobs have very different runtime and parallelism behavior.
ai.approach_zh: It uses the stepwise structure of diffusion inference to enable step-boundary preemption and pairs that with adaptive parallelism, dynamic batching, and an SLO-aware global scheduler.
ai.evidence_zh: The abstract reports experimental gains of up to 44% in SLO attainment rate versus a strongest baseline across diverse configurations, but it does not make the evaluation setup or tradeoffs clear.
ai.value_zh: If the method is robust, it could improve utilization and tail-latency control for mixed diffusion serving clusters without changing the underlying generation models.
open_questions: What hardware setup, workload mix, and SLO definitions were used to produce the reported SLO attainment gains?
How much overhead do step-level preemption, elastic sequence parallelism, and dynamic batching introduce in practice?
How fair are the baselines, especially regarding batching, parallelism configuration, and scheduling knowledge about diffusion step structure?
core_contributions: Frames diffusion co-serving around step-level predictability and preemptibility rather than treating requests as monolithic jobs.
Combines three coordinated mechanisms: intelligent video preemption, elastic sequence parallelism with dynamic batching, and an SLO-aware scheduler.
Targets heterogeneous T2I and T2V co-serving as a systems problem and reports improved SLO attainment over prior serving baselines.
why_read: Useful if you care about scheduler and runtime design for mixed generative workloads on shared GPU clusters.
The step-boundary preemption idea is a concrete systems lever that may generalize to other iterative inference pipelines.
The paper appears focused on latency/SLO behavior rather than model changes, which is relevant for serving-stack readers.
risks: The abstract emphasizes SLO attainment but does not clarify throughput, utilization, or cost tradeoffs.
It is unclear whether the reported gains depend on specific workload predictability or carefully tuned cluster settings that may limit generality after a full read.
recommended_for: Researchers working on inference scheduling and multi-tenant GPU serving.
Engineers operating shared clusters for diffusion image and video generation.
Readers interested in runtime mechanisms for preemption and adaptive parallelism in iterative inference.
ai.reading_priority_reason: This is relevant to serving efficiency and scheduler design, but it is adjacent to the user's core LLM and low-level code generation focus, and the abstract leaves important systems details unspecified.
ai.fallback_summary: Diffusion models have emerged as the prevailing approach for text-to-image (T2I) and text-to-video (T2V) generation, yet production platforms must increasingly serve both modalities on shared GPU clusters while meeting stringent latency SLOs.

top_papers.title: Agentic Code Optimization via Compiler-LLM Cooperation
top_papers.summary: Generating performant executables from high level languages is critical to software performance across a wide range of domains. Modern compilers perform this task by passing code through a series of well-studied optimizations at progressively lower levels of abstraction, but may miss optimization opportunities that require high-level reasoning about a program's purpose. Recent work has proposed using LLMs to fill this gap. While LLMs can achieve large speedups on some programs, they frequently generate code that is incorrect. In this work, we propose a method to balance the correctness of conventional compiler optimizations with the ``creativity'' of LLM-based code generation: compiler-LLM cooperation. Our approach integrates existing compiler optimization passes with LLM-based code generation at multiple levels of abstraction, retaining the best features of both types of code optimization. We realize our approach with a multi-agent system that includes (1) LLM-based optimization agents for each level of abstraction, (2) individual compiler constituents as tools, (3) an LLM-based test generation agent that probes the correctness and performance of generated code, and (4) a guiding LLM that orchestrates the other components. The strategy enables LLM-based optimization of input programs at multiple levels of abstraction and introduces a method for distributing computational budget between levels. Our extensive evaluation shows that compiler-LLM cooperation outperforms both existing compiler optimizations and level-specific LLM-based baselines, producing speedups up to 1.25x.

ai.one_liner_zh: This paper proposes a compiler-LLM optimization pipeline that inserts LLM agents at multiple abstraction levels and uses testing plus orchestration to trade off aggressive rewrites against correctness.
ai.summary_zh: This paper targets a real gap in program optimization: standard compiler passes are reliable but can miss opportunities that require higher-level semantic reasoning, while LLM rewrites can sometimes find better code but often break correctness. The proposed system combines both by placing LLM optimization agents alongside compiler tools at multiple abstraction levels, with a guiding LLM coordinating the process and a separate LLM agent generating tests to check correctness and performance. The abstract claims that this cooperative setup beats conventional compiler optimization and single-level LLM baselines, with reported speedups up to 1.25x. The practical value is the attempt to make LLM-driven optimization less brittle by constraining it inside a compiler-oriented workflow rather than treating code rewriting as a free-form generation problem. The biggest missing detail is evaluation quality: the abstract does not make clear what programs were used, how correctness was validated beyond generated tests, or how much compute budget the multi-agent search requires.
ai.background_zh: Compiler pipelines are strong at systematic low-level optimization, but they are less suited to transformations that depend on higher-level intent, which is why LLM-based optimization has recently been explored despite correctness risks.
ai.problem_zh: The core problem is how to use LLMs for program optimization without giving up the reliability of compiler-driven code generation.
ai.approach_zh: The method interleaves compiler passes and LLM optimization agents across multiple abstraction levels, adds an LLM test-generation component for validation, and uses a guiding LLM to allocate search effort across levels.
ai.evidence_zh: The abstract supports the claim that the system was evaluated against compiler-only and level-specific LLM baselines and reports speedups up to 1.25x, but it does not make the evaluation setup or validation strength clear.
ai.value_zh: If the method is robust, it could provide a more disciplined way to use LLMs for performance optimization by embedding them inside a compiler-and-testing loop rather than relying on unconstrained code rewrites.
open_questions: What benchmarks and program classes were used, and do the gains come from a few outliers or hold broadly across workloads?
How is semantic correctness established in cases where LLM-generated tests may miss corner cases or undefined-behavior changes?
What is the runtime and token-cost overhead of the multi-agent orchestration, and does the optimization payoff justify that budget?
core_contributions: A compiler-LLM cooperation framework that mixes standard compiler passes with LLM-driven optimization instead of replacing the compiler outright.
A multi-agent design with separate optimization agents at different abstraction levels plus a guiding controller.
An LLM-based test-generation component used to probe correctness and performance of generated variants.
why_read: It is one of the more systems-oriented attempts to make LLM code optimization compatible with compiler structure and validation.
It frames optimization as budgeted search across abstraction levels, which is more concrete than single-shot rewrite generation.
It may be relevant to readers interested in whether LLMs can contribute to low-level optimization without fully trusting generated code.
risks: The reported gain ceiling in the abstract is modest, so orchestration overhead may matter.
Correctness assurance may be weak if it depends heavily on generated tests rather than stronger validation methods.
recommended_for: Engineers evaluating LLMs for performance-sensitive code transformation
Readers interested in low-level code generation with correctness constraints
ai.reading_priority_reason: The paper is aligned with compiler and low-level code generation themes, but it is less directly tied to LLM inference acceleration or CUDA kernel generation, and the abstract leaves important evaluation and reproducibility details unclear.
ai.fallback_summary: Generating performant executables from high level languages is critical to software performance across a wide range of domains.

daily_brief.overview_zh: Today's main threads are training and inference efficiency, agents and active exploration. The set is anchored by TriAttention: Efficient Long… and GENSERVE: Efficient Co-Serving of…. Agentic Code Optimization via… adds another data point on whether the same line of work carries stronger evidence or engineering value.
reading_strategy: Start with TriAttention: Efficient Long… and verify whether it defines the training and inference efficiency problem clearly and exposes a real mechanism.
Then read GENSERVE: Efficient Co-Serving of… to see whether the novelty comes from model design, training procedure, or evaluation reframing.
Use Agentic Code Optimization via… as a final cross-check on experimental boundaries and practical value.
